captions.text: So, I played the extremely drearily titled  Directive 8020, the new game by Supermassive,
creators of The Quarry and The Dark Pictures  Anthology. In other news, I'm gonna give it
five more minutes before I decide if I'm going  to take my hand off this hot stove. But you know
what? Awful title aside, Directive 8020 does  show a bit of improvement. And in other news,
the hot stove doesn't hurt quite as much as  it used to, although thinking about it, that
could be nerve damage. Seriously, though, I keep  coming back because I figure either Supermassive
will eventually get better with practice at making  their idiosyncratic, branching-path, narrative,
disappointing choose-your-own-adventure  book games; or I'll have another lovely,
horribly written game to get good and worked up  about and I can tell my doctor I got my cardio
in this week, so win-win. Directive 8020 is a  new instalment of the Dark Pictures Anthology,
although apparently nobody told the Steam page  that; I didn't notice a single reference to Dark
Pictures anywhere in the copy until I actually  started the game and a Dark Pictures logo faded
in as if to say, "Tee hee! Fooled you!" What's  the matter, Supermassive Games? Is Dark Pictures
not becoming the hot, instant-sell,  household name you were hoping for?
No, of course not. New audiences would say, "I  won't play that; I haven't caught up with the
previous episodes." And old audiences would  say, "I won't play that; it's probably shit."
Whatever. I called in my Man of Medan review that  the Dark Pictures series was never in a million
years gonna get to the promised eight episodes,  so I'm taking this as a partial win. Directive
90210 is Supermassive's take on the classic hard  sci-fi Alien/The Thing premise—that is to say,
an awfully generic version of it. Number of  astronauts stumble upon thing in deep space;
turns out thing has a nark on; story ends with  significantly smaller number of astronauts. And
hasn't this been a fucking theme lately? Pragmata,  Saros, Aphelion; when are these astronauts gonna
learn to just leave a "sorry we missed you" card  on the evil alien planet and bugger off home?
Our story opens focusing on two technicians  tasked with looking after the rest of the
crew while they're in hypersleep for a four-year  journey. And immediately we see exactly how much
Supermassive's writing has improved because  these dudes who've been alone together for
four years in tight quarters are still having to  explain their fucking personalities to each other.
"I'm determined to focus on the importance of  our mission and the future of humanity." No,
no, no. "What up, shithead?" "Suck my dick,  fuckface." THAT's how they'd be communicating at
that point. Anyway, long prologue short, an evil  alien fungus thing gets onboard and possesses the
one you're not controlling. Which was obvious to  me, so I took the branching path option that led
to my dude running away rather than the one  that led to him getting a wrench to the face,
but how that looked in context was that homeslice  ran away in panting mortal terror because his
coworker looked at him weird. After that, the  rest of the crew are woken up for their mission,
notice the technicians behaving oddly, and events  slowly spiral into paranoia, infighting, desperate
survival, and dodgy facial animation. The story  is presented with the typical awkwardness of
Supermassive interactive movies, with only a  generous 30 to 50% of the cast actually knowing
how to act for shit, and they still come across  weird and stilted 'cause of all the obvious cut
points between lines. And I saw multiple scenes  where a character walks into a room, wordlessly
does the thing they need to do there, then walks  out again. 'Cause some choice I made earlier
removed the interesting thing that was supposed  to happen. Or I'd killed off the character who
was supposed to have a line there criticizing  the other character's "doing a thing" technique.
Before long, it becomes clear to both us and the  cast that the alien fungus is doing the thing from
The Thing and creating evil clones of the humans  to infiltrate their ranks and spread paranoia
and discord. The player character is repeatedly  forced to choose which of two copies to trust,
and you can never be sure which one is real.  Unless you're the kind of investigative genius who
notices things like inconsistent stories, altered  speech patterns, and the big fat achievement that
popped at one point reading, "Congratulations!  You made it to act 6 without falling for a single
imposter!" Well, so much for fucking suspense,  eh, game? You can also spoil yourself quite easily
by examining the timeline menu too closely 'cause  you can look at the icon for the locked alternate
path, and it'll say, "Unlock this path by being  a big dum dum and trusting an imposter like you
didn't do the first time around." But I don't  wanna keep harping on all the problems that one
could argue are inherent to the format because  what the hell do I expect Supermassive to do
about that, NOT make choose-your-own-adventure  book games? At this point, that's like asking
my dog to stop licking his bum. Completely  futile. These days, I consider myself lucky
if he just doesn't do it directly behind my  head while I'm streaming with the webcam on.
So let's focus on what we absolutely  can't blame on awkwardly edited timelines,
such as still lacking the ability to FUCKING  SKIP CUTSCENES. There are so many areas you
could stand to improve in, Supermassive, but this  one is the fucking gimme. It would be so easy.
You can't expect players to want to go back and  try every path to get the 100% completion rating
AND expect them to never get bored of watching  these twats wibbleface their way through the
same lines over and over again. On that subject,  and the subject of things that are slightly less
easy to fix, the writing still sucks overpriced  food out of the stomach cavity of a beloved,
recently deceased pet. "Hey, the ship's interior  is packed with an evil alien fungus that keeps
creating murderous mimics of us that can pop  up literally anywhere if we take our eyes off
each other even for a second. Right, now that's  established, let's split up and all go off alone
for the 19th fucking time." And you're no better,  evil alien fungus. "Ooh, look at me. I can create
perfect copies of my prey and even convincingly  talk to them in their own language and have almost
successfully fooled them on multiple occasions.  But I'm bored of doing that now, so I'll just
create a bunch of mindless, animalistic,  gribbly monsters for the last few acts."
Sorry, I've just remembered way back in the mists  of time I was planning to praise this game at some
point. Well, it improves upon Supermassive's  record in the following areas: the cast are
all grown-up professionals rather than hateful,  snarky teenagers; the enclosed spaceship setting
works a lot better with the usual "and then  there were none" storyline and gives a coherent,
consistent environment we can watch evolve over  time as the alien fills more and more of it with
wibbly-wobbly purple badness goo; and this reduces  the usual Supermassive problem with being confused
about the geography of every scene and where every  character is supposed to be from shot to shot to
a mere 60 to 70% of the runtime. And there's  a greater commitment to moderately involved,
honest-to-goodness gameplay besides the  fucking quick time events. Usually, admittedly,
in the form of fucking mandatory stealth  sections, but I'll take it at this point. So,
hey, for whatever it's worth, I enjoyed  Directive 867-5309 more than I did the
fucking Quarry at least. There's hope for you  yet, Supermassive Games. All you have to do now
is replace all your story writers. And most of  your gameplay writers. And some of your artists.
And call yourself something different as  well. I didn't say it was a LOT of hope.
